exercises.title: A: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
I found an old calculator by chance in the cupboard. **across**
I ...... an old calculator in the cupboard.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is find by chance, so the correct answer is "came across".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "came across", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Tablets and laptops are not exactly the same. **difference**
There ...... tablets and laptops.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not the same, so the correct answer is "is a difference between".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is a difference between", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Inventors should never invent false information. **made**
False information should never ...... inventors.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive, so the correct answer is "be made up by".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Don't put those old batteries in the bin – recycle them! **away**
Don't ...... – recycle them!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is dispose of, so the correct answer is "throw those old batteries away".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "throw those old batteries away", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The printer stopped working during the lesson, so we used another one. **down**
We used another printer when ours ...... during the lesson.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is stop working, so the correct answer is "broke down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "broke down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The scientist's discovery caused a change in medical research. **resulted**
The scientist's ...... a change in medical research.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is led to, so the correct answer is "discovery resulted in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "discovery resulted in", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
There are several gadgets that I would like to test. **number**
There are ...... gadgets that I would like to test.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is several, so the correct answer is "a number of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Make sure you put clean water in the tank before turning on the machine. **full**
Make sure the tank ...... clean water before turning on the machine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is contains, so the correct answer is "is full of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is full of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: B: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Please turn the computer ...... before you leave the classroom.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is switch off, so the correct answer is "off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "off" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Please turn the computer ...... before you leave the classroom.".

items.sentence: ...... my opinion, this gadget is too complicated.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is opinion, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Disconnect the charger ...... the tablet when the battery is full.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is separate from, so the correct answer is "from".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: We had three possible answers, but ...... the end only one was correct.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is finally, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: ...... last, the scientists discovered what had caused the problem.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is at last, so the correct answer is "at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: How many types ...... software have you used this year?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Types of software.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "of" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "How many types ...... software have you used this year?".

items.sentence: It's getting cold, so I'll turn ...... the heater.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is switch on, so the correct answer is "on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: There must be a reason ...... the sudden decrease in speed.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Reason for the decrease.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: C: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The ...... (LONG) of the bridge is over two kilometres.
items.explanation: Noun from LONG → **length** of the bridge.

items.sentence: The lecture about artificial intelligence was really ...... (FASCINATE).
The two robots moved almost ...... (IDENTICAL).
The researchers reached a surprising ...... (CONCLUDE).

items.sentenceAfter: created the first version of the device.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "who" — the inventor = person.

wrongOptions.A: "which" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "who" is correct.
wrongOptions.which: "which" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "who" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "whose" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "who" is correct.
wrongOptions.whose: "whose" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "who" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "whom" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "who" is correct.
wrongOptions.whom: "whom" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "who" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "who" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: we visited yesterday has a robotics exhibition.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "which" — the museum = place/thing.

wrongOptions.A: "who" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.who: "who" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.B: "whose" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.whose: "whose" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.D: "whom" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.whom: "whom" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When you see a finished past-time expression, reach for the past simple first.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: A finished past time marker (yesterday, last week, ago) requires the past simple, not a perfect tense.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is possession, so the correct answer is "whose".

wrongOptions.A: "who" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "whose" is correct.
wrongOptions.who: "who" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "whose" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "which" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "whose" is correct.
wrongOptions.which: "which" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "whose" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "that" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "whose" is correct.
wrongOptions.that: "that" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "whose" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Possessive forms and possessive determiners show who something belongs to.

items.sentenceAfter: I found about the new invention.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "that" — everything = thing.

wrongOptions.A: "who" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "that" is correct.
wrongOptions.who: "who" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "that" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "which" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "that" is correct.
wrongOptions.which: "which" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "that" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "what" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "that" is correct.
wrongOptions.what: "what" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "that" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

exercises.title: E: Complete the sentences
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: The university is carrying ...... research into artificial intelligence.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is do research, so the correct answer is "out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "out" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "The university is carrying ...... research into artificial intelligence.".

items.sentence: The invention of the smartphone had a huge ...... on daily life.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is strong effect, so the correct answer is "impact".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence: Maya came ...... with a clever idea for saving water.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is think of an idea, so the correct answer is "up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The test failed the first time, so they tried it ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is once more, so the correct answer is "again".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: He worked ...... how to repair the old radio.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is solve, so the correct answer is "out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Researchers ...... a new material that is lighter than plastic.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is find something new, so the correct answer is "discovered".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The students were amazed ...... the results of the experiment.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is surprised by, so the correct answer is "at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: They decided to ...... for a patent before selling the gadget.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is request officially, so the correct answer is "apply".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
